comments.text: I thought I'd remark that Erdős asked this problem at least a couple more times before the stated reference [Er83d, p. 323]: here (p. 122-123) in 1978-79 and here (p. 30) in 1981. Also, R. Daniel Mauldin wrote about the problem in this paper from 2001 (Section 1) and this paper from 2013 (Section 5). However, the problem still seems to be open. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

One useful fact stated in Mauldin's paper is the following: this problem is equivalent to the one in which we restrict attention to sets $A$ that are unions of finitely many closed disks of equal radius. Indeed, by the inner regularity of the Lebesgue measure, it suffices to consider only compact sets $A$. After covering $A$ by $\varepsilon$-disks centered at points of $A$, one can apply Besicovitch's covering theorem to obtain the desired equivalence.

Even slightly better, a further reduction is easy to see: one can assume that $A$ is a finite union of congruent dyadic squares, which are sets of the form\[ \big[2^{-n}k,2^{-n}(k+1)\big) \times\big[2^{-n}l,2^{-n}(l+1)\big) \]for $k,l,n\in\mathbb{Z}$. They are better because they are disjoint and correspond to the integer lattice $\mathbb{Z}^2$. After scaling by $2^n$, one can essentially think about the corresponding discrete problem: can one find $\omega(4^n)$ points inside the grid $[4^n]\times[4^n]$ such that no three of them form a triangle of area $\approx \alpha$ for some fixed $\alpha$ of the order $4^n$. I'm not being precise here, I'm just saying that constructions of such large discrete sets would lead to the counterexample to the main problem and vice versa. Some careless discrete reformulations can go wrong, of course, e.g., if one targets the precise area $\alpha$ which is an odd number, while $A$ consists of points with both even coordinates.

Nice reduction! Based on your idea, I formulated a precise translation into a discrete problem. For an integer $N>0$, we say that a set of lattice points $S\subset \mathbb{Z}^2$ is $N$-avoiding if for every $q_1,q_2,q_3\in S$ we have\[ \bigl\lvert \mu(\triangle q_1q_2q_3) - N \bigr\rvert > \mathrm{diam}(\triangle q_1q_2q_3). \]Let $f(N)$ denote the largest possible size of an $N$-avoiding set. Then the original question is equivalent to asking whether $f(N)=O(N)$ holds. For a proof of the equivalence, please see here.

This is very nice and worth writing down. However, your discrete reformulation is combinatorially complicated and I still find it beneficial to play with simplified discrete models to gain intuition about the problem.

I prepared a public repository of verified lower-bound witnesses for $N=1,\dots,40$ in Koizumi's discrete $N$-avoiding model related to Erdős Problem #352. Repository: erdos352-koizumi-n-avoiding-witness-table Interactive viewer: open the HTML witness viewer The entries $N=1,\dots,27$ are based on BorisAlexeev's posted witnesses in this thread, converted into a uniform JSON format and reverified. The entries $N=28,\dots,40$ are additional witnesses found by my search program. All witnesses are checked by an exact integer verifier included in the repository. Current lower bounds: N=1..10: 1, 4, 4, 7, 9, 10, 12, 14, 16, 18 N=11..20: 21, 23, 24, 26, 28, 32, 32, 34, 38, 39 N=21..30: 41, 44, 45, 48, 52, 52, 54, 56, 60, 61 N=31..40: 63, 65, 69, 69, 72, 74, 76, 78, 80, 82 Corrections, independent checks, or better witnesses are very welcome. AI usage disclosure: This comment was prepared with assistance from Codex 5.5 using xhigh reasoning, and ChatGPT 5.5 Pro.

Following Koizumi's discrete $N$-avoiding lattice formulation above, I want to record a small observation about the low-side subproblem. The theorem I want to state is the following. Let $S$ be a finite subset of $\mathbb{Z}^2$. I will say that $S$ is low-side for $N$ if every triple $a,b,c\in S$ satisfies $\operatorname{area}(\triangle abc)+\operatorname{diam}(\triangle abc)<N$. Let $F_{\mathrm{low}}(N)$ be the maximum possible size of such a set. Then $F_{\mathrm{low}}(N)=(4\pi/\sqrt{27})N+O(\sqrt{N})$. Thus, in the low-side part of Koizumi's model, the disk constant $4\pi/\sqrt{27}$ is the sharp asymptotic constant. The lower bound is the disk construction. If $S$ consists of lattice points in a disk of radius $r$, then every triangle has area at most $(3\sqrt{3}/4)r^2$ and diameter at most $2r$. Choosing $r$ so that $(3\sqrt{3}/4)r^2+2r<N$ gives $|S|=(4\pi/\sqrt{27})N-O(\sqrt{N})$. For the upper bound, let $K=\operatorname{conv}(S)$, let $D=\operatorname{diam}(K)$, and choose a diameter pair $a,b\in S$. Let $W$ be the width of $K$ perpendicular to $ab$. Degenerate collinear cases are immediate, so assume $K$ has positive area. Low-side applied to triangles $abv$, where $v$ is a vertex of $K$, gives $Dh(v)/2+D<N$, where $h(v)$ is the distance from $v$ to the line $ab$. In particular $D<N$ by taking a vertex with positive height, and the same inequalities give $W<4(N-D)/D$. Also $W\le D$. Let $T$ be the maximum area of a triangle in $K$, and let $d$ be the diameter of such a maximum triangle. Since $K$ is a lattice polygon, this maximum triangle may be chosen with vertices in $S$. The triangle lies in a strip of width $W$, so $T\le dW/2$. Low-side gives $T+d<N$, hence $T<N/(1+2/W)$. Now we use the first external theorem. The Blaschke--Sas theorem [1,2] says that every planar convex body $K$ contains a triangle of area at least $(3\sqrt{3}/(4\pi))\operatorname{area}(K)$. Since $T$ is the largest triangle area in $K$, $T\ge (3\sqrt{3}/(4\pi))\operatorname{area}(K)$, i.e. $\operatorname{area}(K)\le (4\pi/\sqrt{27})T$. Next we use Pick's theorem. Let $I$ be the number of lattice points in the interior of $K$, and $B$ the number of lattice points on the boundary of $K$. Pick's theorem [3] gives $\operatorname{area}(K)=I+B/2-1$, so $|K\cap\mathbb{Z}^2|=I+B=\operatorname{area}(K)+B/2+1$. Since $B\le \operatorname{perim}(K)$, and $S\subset K\cap\mathbb{Z}^2$, we get $|S|\le \operatorname{area}(K)+\operatorname{perim}(K)/2+1$. Also $K$ is contained in a $D$ by $W$ rectangle, so $\operatorname{perim}(K)\le 2D+2W$ and hence $\operatorname{perim}(K)/2\le D+W$. Therefore $|S|\le (4\pi/\sqrt{27})N/(1+2/W)+D+W+O(1)$. It remains to split into two cases. Write $c=4\pi/\sqrt{27}$. If $D\le 2\sqrt{N}$, then $W\le D\le 2\sqrt{N}$ and $cN/(1+2/W)\le cN$, so $|S|\le cN+D+W+O(1)\le cN+O(\sqrt{N})$. If $D>2\sqrt{N}$, put $W_0=4(N-D)/D$. Then $W<W_0<4N/D<2\sqrt{N}$, so $W=O(\sqrt{N})$. It remains to show $cN/(1+2/W)+D\le cN$. Since $cN/(1+2/W)=cNW/(W+2)$ is increasing in $W$, $\frac{cN}{1+2/W}+D\le \frac{cN}{1+2/W_0}+D=cN\frac{2(N-D)}{2N-D}+D=cN-\frac{cND}{2N-D}+D$. As noted above, $D<N$. Also $c>2$, so $cN>2N>2N-D$, and hence $cND/(2N-D)\ge D$. Therefore the last displayed quantity is at most $cN$, and $|S|\le cN+W+O(1)\le cN+O(\sqrt{N})$. References. [1] Sas, E. Über eine Extremumeigenschaft der Ellipsen. Compositio Mathematica 6 (1939), 468--470. https://numdam.org/item/CM_1939__6__468_0/ [2] Balko, M.; Jelinek, V.; Valtr, P.; Walczak, B.On the Beer Index of Convexity and Its Variants. Discrete & Computational Geometry 57 (2017), 179--214 https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s00454-016-9821-3 [3] Pick, G. Geometrisches zur Zahlenlehre. Lotos - Zeitschrift fuer Naturwissenschaften 47, Neue Folge XIX (1899), 311--319. https://www.zobodat.at/pdf/Lotos_47_0311-0319.pdf AI usage disclosure: This comment was prepared with assistance from Codex 5.5 using xhigh reasoning, and ChatGPT 5.5 Pro.

I made an attempt at a finite upper-bound scheme for $f(N)$ in Koizumi's discrete $N$-avoiding lattice model for Erdős #352. Start with an arbitrary $N$-avoiding set $S$ and choose a diameter pair in $S$. After translation and symmetry, write it as $A=(0,0)$ and $B=(a,b)$, and put $D=|A-B|$. For this fixed diameter vector $B$, the remaining candidates $q$ are finite. The first test is the triangle $A,B,q$. A point $q$ survives only if $|\operatorname{area}(A,B,q)-N|>D$. So each surviving $q$ is classified as follows: low if $\operatorname{area}(A,B,q)<N-D$, high if $\operatorname{area}(A,B,q)>N+D$. Points with $N-D\le\operatorname{area}(A,B,q)\le N+D$ are discarded. The compatibility graph is built as follows. Its vertices are the remaining candidates $q$. An edge $q_iq_j$ means that $q_i$ and $q_j$ are still allowed to be chosen together with the fixed points. In the no-high branch the fixed points are $A,B$, so the pair $q_i,q_j$ is tested together with $A$ and with $B$, for example by checking the triples $A,q_i,q_j$ and $B,q_i,q_j$. In the high-side branch the fixed points are $A,B,h$, so the triple $h,q_i,q_j$ is also checked. For this $B$, I calculate two branch bounds: no-high branch: let $G_B^0$ be the compatibility graph on the low candidates. This branch gives $2+\chi(G_B^0)$. high-side branch: after fixing one high point $h$, let $G_{B,h}$ be the compatibility graph on the remaining survivors. This branch gives $3+\chi(G_{B,h})$. This graph calculation is intentionally relaxed: it does not check every triple $q_i,q_j,q_k$ made only from unfixed candidates. Therefore the color count is not claimed to be the exact maximum size of an avoiding set. It is only an upper bound. The reason it is still valid is that every actual avoiding choice gives a clique in the compatibility graph, and $\omega(G)\le\chi(G)$. The case bound for this $B$ is the larger of the no-high bound and all high-side bounds. Finally, the global upper bound for $f(N)$ is the maximum over all normalized diameter vectors $B$. I checked the implementation for $N=1,\ldots,7$: N: 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 upper bound: 1 4 4 7 9 10 12 These match the known lower-bound witnesses in these small cases. For $N>7$, the current readable implementation became too slow for me to complete the full runs, so I am reporting only these completed checks. GitHub: https://github.com/KitaKen1/erdos352-koizumi-fn-upper-bound AI usage disclosure: the code and writeup were prepared with assistance from Codex 5.5 and ChatGPT 5.5 Pro.

Related to the above discretization, I can perhaps suggest the following discrete toy-problem, which, I believe, captures the essence of the original question: Let $f(N)$ be cardinality of the largest set $A\subseteq[N]\times[N]$ such that no three points from $A$ span the triangle of area $\in[0.99N,1.01N]$. Is $f(N)=O(N)$? This, at least in theory, leaves room for computer experimentation, to perhaps try to find the "worst" arrangements, or to investigate all "types" of possible behaviors. There is some superficial similarity with the No-three-in-line problem, which could be considered as the search for triangles of area 0. However, $f(N)\leq2N$ is obvious there, unlike in this problem.

It's not much of an idea, but it might be useful to first play with triangles which have one side parallel to the $x$-axis. Namely, either in continuous or discrete setting, if $A$ does not contain such a triple of points spanning a triangle of area $1$ and if $B$ is its projection onto the $y$-axis, then each (one-dimensional) horizontal section $A_y := A \cap (\mathbb{R}\times\{y\})$ (viewed as a subset of $\mathbb{R}$) only has to avoid certain differences (determined by $B$ and $y$). Inside the grid $[N]\times [N]$ this leads to a more effective algorithm for finding the largest allowed $A\subseteq[N]\times[N]$, of complexity $O(2^{2N})$, rather than $O(2^{N^2})$. Using this, I computed, for example, sizes of the largest $A\subseteq[N]\times[N]$ avoiding, say, areas $(N-1)/2$, $N/2$, $(N+1)/2$: grid 2x2, forbidden areas = {1/2,1,3/2}, maximum points = 2, grid 3x3, forbidden areas = {1,3/2,2}, maximum points = 4, grid 4x4, forbidden areas = {3/2,2,5/2}, maximum points = 7, grid 5x5, forbidden areas = {2,5/2,3}, maximum points = 8, grid 6x6, forbidden areas = {5/2,3,7/2}, maximum points = 12, grid 7x7, forbidden areas = {3,7/2,4}, maximum points = 16, grid 8x8, forbidden areas = {7/2,4,9/2}, maximum points = 22, grid 9x9, forbidden areas = {4,9/2,5}, maximum points = 25, grid 10x10, forbidden areas = {9/2,5,11/2}, maximum points = 38. These experimental results still don't say much, since this discrete problem is too sensitive on divisibility issues. For instance, there is no way that a triangle with vertices in $[10]\times[10]$ can have area $11/2$, so this condition is not restricting anything.

Aleksandar Bulj and I made partial progress on this problem of a different nature in this preprint. One can ask for an upper bound on the measure of every set $A$ contained in a large square $[0,R]^2$ that avoids every triple of points spanning a triangle of area 1. Erdős's conjecture is, of course, that it can be $O(1)$, i.e., uniform in $R$. However, while this is still open, one can still concentrate on upper bounds in terms of $R\to\infty$, i.e., prove the so-called "density theorem" in this context, anything better than the trivial $O(R^2)$. Aleksandar and I showed an upper bound of the form\[ O\Big( R^2 \Big(\frac{\log\log R}{\log R}\Big)^{1/2} \Big). \]Namely, we couldn't find any concrete upper bound in the literature. There is only a very weak $o(R^2)$ that can be deduced from a brief discrete comment by Graham, which we also deciphered in our Appendix B. Our proof uses quite a lot of tools from harmonic analysis (trilinear smoothing, induction on scales, etc.). It would be interesting to see if a better upper bound can be shown using other ideas. Our paper would survive even if the full Erdős problem was solved, because we were also concerned with a more rigid configuration, where $O(1)$ is not possible (and we get a slightly worse upper bound). Was AI used? Yes, ChatGPT 5.5 Pro helped us increase the exponent, from 1/3 to 1/2. It also helped us decipher Graham's comment.

To ask an obvious question: can someone give a set that is larger than the appropriately-sized circle (open disk)? I calculate the radius of that circle as $r=\frac{2}{3^{3/4}}$ and its area as $c=\frac{4\pi}{\sqrt{27}} \approx 2.418$. (I was not able to access [Er83d].)
Indeed, Erdős himself in [Er83d] guessed that perhaps $C=4\pi/\sqrt{27}$ works for the same reason. (He also said that 'if true this conjecture should perhaps not be difficult to prove'.)

In the first comment above, I linked to two sources older than [Er83d]: they are both freely accessible. In the first one, from 1978, Erdős already mentions precisely that constant as potentially working. (Mauldin, see above, also played with this stronger version of the conjecture.) I don't remember seeing that even this value of $c$ has been disproved somewhere. However, one must be aware that there are numerous "local extremizers" for the problem: there is a sequence of area-1-triangle-avoiding sets $(A_N)_{N=1}^{\infty}$ with areas $\gg1$ but arbitrarily large diameters. For instance, for each $(m,n)\in[N]\times[N]$ take a disk around $(2m,2n)$ of radius $\frac{1}{100N}$ and let $A_N$ be the union of all these disks.

FWIW, Sas’s theorem implies that any convex set of area greater than $c = 4\pi/\sqrt{27}$ contains a triangle of area $1$. Moreover, Mauldin's 2013 article mentioned in Vjeko's comment shows that the same constant $c$ is optimal even for unions of at most three convex sets.
